@graph.name: Formal Demonstration of the Golden Ratio from a Given Line
@graph.description: A structured reconstruction of the geometric derivation of φ using Euclid-inspired constructions, including an alternative to Elements II.11 and the inscription of a regular decagon.

@graph.name: Extended Proposition II.11.1
@graph.text: To add to a given straight line a segment such that the square on the whole is equal to the rectangle contained by the sum of both lines and the added segment.
steps: Let AB be the given line. We seek a segment s such that AB² equals (AB + s)·s.
Construct square ABCD on AB (Elements I.46).
Bisect AB at point Γ and draw ΓC.
Extend AB to Δ so that ΓΔ = ΓC.
Construct rectangle AΔ, ΔH such that ΔH = BΔ.
Using Elements II.6, the square of ΓB plus rectangle AΔ, ΔH equals the square of ΓΔ.
Using Elements I.47, the square of ΓC equals the square of BC plus the square of ΓB.
Since BC = AB and ΓC = ΓΔ, rectangle AΔ, ΔH equals AB².
Since AΔ = AB + s and ΔH = s, we obtain (AB + s)·s = AB².
Q.E.D.

@graph.name: Extended Proposition IV.11.1
@graph.text: To inscribe an equilateral and equiangular decagon in a given circle using the segment constructed in Proposition II.11.1.
steps: Let AB be the given line, used as the radius r of circle C.
Add segment BΔ such that AB² = AΔ·ΔB, obtaining the length AΔ = AB + s.
Draw circle C with center O and radius AB.
Choose any point E on circle C.
Draw a circle with center E and radius AΔ.
Use each new intersection point with circle C as a new center to draw circles of radius AΔ.
Continue until no new intersections appear; exactly ten points are obtained.
Connect the ten points to form the decagon.
The decagon is equilateral because no additional intersections can be generated.
The decagon is equiangular because each pair of consecutive vertices and the center O form congruent isosceles triangles.
Q.E.D.
